name.note: No passenger manifest, census household, or church record yet found naming Herman's father. This is the primary open gap.

born.place: Germany — most probable province: Kingdom of Saxony (see province_assessment)

immigration.reasoning: Herman born 1863 in Texas; therefore parents arrived before 1863. 1850 Lavaca County census shows no Boehme households (family not yet present). Immigration window calculated accordingly.
immigration.port_of_entry: Indianola, Calhoun County, Texas (probable) or Galveston (possible)
immigration.rationale: Indianola was primary German immigrant port pre-1875; Old Indianola Trail ran to Yorktown (DeWitt County) → Lavaca County. Indianola destroyed by hurricane 1875 — family arrived well before.
immigration.ship: UNKNOWN — Hamburg passenger lists 1855-1862 (Ancestry collection 1068 / FamilySearch Klüber Index) not yet searched for Boehme/Böhme

province_assessment.upgrade_rationale: Multiple converging lines of evidence now support Saxony as the primary candidate province.

evidence.finding: Böhme surname concentrates 34% in Saxony, 11% in Saxony-Anhalt, 9% in North Rhine-Westphalia. Saxony is by far the #1 German state.
Texas has 15% of all US Boehme bearers — the highest concentration of any state, consistent with a single significant Texas-German immigration cluster.

evidence.item: Collateral Boehme Texas immigrant — Friedrich August Boehme (1809-1871)
evidence.finding: Born Waldenburg, Landkreis Zwickau, Saxony → died Castroville, Medina County TX. Son Armin born 1853 in Texas. A physician (Dr.) — confirms an educated Saxon immigrant in the exact Texas-German corridor. Waldenburg is in Zwickau district, Kingdom of Saxony.
evidence.weight: STRONG — confirms Saxony-to-Texas pipeline for Boehme surname, same 1850s era
evidence.note: This is NOT Herman's father (Medina County ≠ Lavaca County, and children's names don't match). But proves the Waldenburg/Saxony → Texas migration route for this exact surname.

evidence.finding: Böhme/Boehme = 'one from Bohemia' (Böhmen). The surname originated among ethnic Germans who left Bohemia (now Czech Republic) for Saxony — primarily during and after the Thirty Years War (1618-1648) when Protestant/Hussite refugees fled Bohemia and settled in neighboring Saxony.
evidence.source: Webster's Dictionary of American Family Names; HouseOfNames.com; historical surname research

evidence.finding: Breslau TX (Lavaca County, 7 miles NW of Hallettsville) is named after Breslau (Wroclaw), capital of Prussian Silesia. TSHA confirms Lutheran church in this community. Suggests some Silesian immigrants also settled in Lavaca County.
evidence.weight: SUPPLEMENTARY — consistent with either Saxony or Silesia origin

evidence.finding: The most famous historical Böhme (theologian, 1575-1624) was born in Alt-Seidenberg, Niederschlesien (Lower Silesia), near Görlitz — exactly the Saxony-Silesia border region. Concentrations of his surname in Saxony reflect descendants of his region.
Missouri Synod (LCMS) Lutheran roots are Saxon — founders were Saxon Lutheran emigrants of 1838-1839. Herman's probable LCMS affiliation is further consistent with Saxon origin.

alternative.rationale: Breslau TX place name; Böhme surname also present in Silesian border region; prior agent 45 assessed Silesia as most probable. Saxony and Silesia share a border and many immigrants had mixed provincial origins.

provinces_excluded: Bavaria — Catholic dominant; no Catholic markers in Boehme Lavaca County cluster
Nassau/Holstein/Oldenburg — Adelsverein wave; wrong settlement zone
Bohemia proper — Herman was ethnic German Lutheran, not Czech/Bohemian; Böhme surname = Germans FROM Bohemia, not Bohemians

specific_locality_candidate.name: Waldenburg, Landkreis Zwickau, Kingdom of Saxony
specific_locality_candidate.confidence: POSSIBLE — based on collateral Friedrich August Boehme immigrant
specific_locality_candidate.note: Cannot confirm Herman's father came from Waldenburg specifically; it remains a geographic candidate. Parish registers for Waldenburg Lutheran church (Stadtkirche Waldenburg) would be the target search.

jakob_bohme.name: Jakob Böhme
jakob_bohme.significance: German Lutheran mystic, theologian, and philosopher. Most famous historical bearer of the surname. His surname reflects that his family were ethnic Germans from the Bohemian borderlands.
jakob_bohme.connection_to_hunter: NONE ESTABLISHED — coincidence of surname only unless documentary lineage is proven
jakob_bohme.note: The concentration of Böhme descendants in Saxony is partly attributable to post-Thirty Years War settlement patterns from his Silesia-Bohemia border region.

settlement_context.region: South Texas German Belt — Lavaca / Wilson / DeWitt / Gonzales Counties
settlement_context.character: Working-class agricultural immigrant families, NOT Adelsverein elite wave
settlement_context.NOT_adelsverein: Confirmed — Adelsverein settlements were Hill Country (Fredericksburg/New Braunfels). Lavaca County is a distinct post-1848 corridor.
settlement_context.immigration_wave: Post-1848 political refugee wave; possibly post-1850 economic emigration
settlement_context.entry_route: Hamburg or Bremen → Atlantic crossing → Indianola (Calhoun County TX) → Old Indianola Trail → Yorktown (DeWitt County) → Lavaca County
settlement_context.fraternal_organization: Order of the Sons of Hermann (Söhne Hermanns) — German Protestant fraternal mutual aid. Herman Boehme buried in Shiner Sons of Hermann Cemetery; lodge chartered Shiner area by 1890s.
settlement_context.church: Lutheran (Saint Johns Lutheran, Poth; Zion Evangelical Lutheran, Moulton; probable Saint Paul Lutheran, Shiner)

key_new_findings_this_research_pass.finding: Minna Marie Macker identified as PROBABLE wife of Herman F. Boehme
key_new_findings_this_research_pass.source: PeopleLegacy via agent 45; now in entities.json
key_new_findings_this_research_pass.significance: Herman's wife / Hilda's mother — previously Unknown

key_new_findings_this_research_pass.finding: Province upgraded from POSSIBLE to PROBABLE Saxony based on Forebears surname frequency data (34% of all Böhme in Germany live in Saxony)
Texas has 15% of all US Boehme bearers — highest concentration of any US state
key_new_findings_this_research_pass.significance: Confirms a meaningful Boehme settlement cluster in Texas relative to the surname's US population

key_new_findings_this_research_pass.finding: Collateral Boehme immigrant confirmed: Dr. Friedrich August Boehme (1809, Waldenburg, Zwickau, Saxony → 1871, Castroville, Medina County TX) with son Armin born 1853 in Texas
key_new_findings_this_research_pass.significance: Proves Saxony (specifically Waldenburg/Zwickau district) → Texas immigration route for Boehme surname in same era. NOT Herman's direct father, but collateral evidence of the migration pattern.

key_new_findings_this_research_pass.finding: Surname etymology: Böhme = ethnic Germans who left Bohemia for Saxony, mainly post-Thirty Years War (1618-1648)
key_new_findings_this_research_pass.significance: Explains why Böhme concentrates in Saxony despite meaning 'Bohemian' — the bearers were Germans resettled in Saxony from Bohemia.

key_new_findings_this_research_pass.finding: Hilda Boehme's birth date refined to 1900-01-08 (PROBABLE from PeopleLegacy)
key_new_findings_this_research_pass.significance: Born 6 months after Herman died (June 18, 1900) — Hilda was possibly posthumous, or born January 1900 before Herman's June death

summary.earliest_confirmed_ancestor: Herman F. Boehme (1863-1900), born Texas, buried Shiner Sons of Hermann Cemetery, Lavaca County TX. Maternal 2x-great-grandfather of Hunter Spence.

summary.key_open_question: G6 Boehme immigrant's name. Highest-yield next step: search 1870 and 1880 Lavaca County TX census on FamilySearch for a Boehme household with Herman (~7 or ~17 years old) and parents born in Germany.
